relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The remaining nations become the arena where untested generations meet the unfinished past.
War is named as something the later generation learns, not something modern readers should sanctify.
Philistine rulers, Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites remain across the land's edges.
Their presence tests whether Israel will listen to the command through Moses.
Israel lives in the midst of many peoples.
Marriage ties and worship ties mingle across covenant boundaries.
Forgetting YHWH becomes service to Baals and Asherahs.
Israel enters eight years under Aram's king.
The cry rises, and Othniel is raised as deliverer.
Spirit rests upon him, and he judges through conflict.
The land breathes forty years until Othniel dies.
The cycle turns again, and Moab is strengthened against Israel.
Moab gathers Ammon and Amalek and takes the city of palms.
Eighteen years pass under Eglon's rule.
The cry rises again, and Ehud's left-handed body becomes the unlikely opening.
He prepares a hidden double-edged blade.
Tribute brings him before Eglon.
Ehud dismisses the carriers.
He returns with a secret word that empties the room.
The claimed word of Divine makes the king rise.
Ehud's left hand draws the blade from the unexpected side.
The assassination is narrated through grotesque bodily detail.
Ehud locks the chamber behind him.
The servants misread the locked doors as privacy.
Delay gives escape time while the dead king lies inside.
Ehud passes the carved stones and reaches safety.
The shofar gathers Israel from Ephraim's hills.
The fords become the place where Moab is trapped.
Ten thousand Moabites fall, and no one escapes.
Moab is subdued, and eighty years of quiet follow.
Shamgar appears briefly, saving Israel through an oxgoad and a body count.

contemplative_note: Judges 3 is liberation told through disturbing tools: war training, assassination, satire, and slaughter. Its holy center is the cry of the oppressed and the raising of help—not the idea that deception or killing becomes clean because it produced quiet.
